era_context: The Early Iron Age is consolidating across the Mediterranean and Near East. Egypt remains internally divided under Ramesses XI. Assyria is in a phase of modest expansion. The Levant is increasingly dominated by local city-states and emerging maritime powers. Greece is in the Geometric period, with iron tools and weapons becoming more common. The transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age technology is ongoing, with iron-working gradually replacing bronze in many regions.

events.title: Continuation of Ramesses XI's reign and internal Egyptian fragmentation
events.description: Ramesses XI's reign continues amid ongoing internal division between the pharaonic authority in Lower Egypt and the High Priests of Amun in Upper Egypt. Administrative records show increasing autonomy of the Theban priesthood. The pharaoh's ability to project power beyond the Nile Valley has effectively ceased. This period represents the final phase of the New Kingdom and the transition toward the Third Intermediate Period.
events.certainty_note: Ramesses XI's reign is well-attested; the specific events of this year are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterisation of this period as one of 'decline' or 'fragmentation' reflects a Eurocentric bias toward centralised state power. From the perspective of local communities and the priesthood, this period may have represented adaptation and the development of alternative power structures rather than simple decline. The notion of a sharp 'New Kingdom/Third Intermediate Period' boundary is a historiographic convenience; the transition was gradual.
historiographic_note: Documentation for this year is minimal. The surviving sources are primarily Egyptian administrative records, which are biased toward recording state activities and priestly concerns. Events outside the Nile Valley and outside the sphere of state activity are almost entirely absent from the record. The perspective of non-elite populations is not represented.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses XI's reign continues; fragmentation deepens
